title: Arthroscopic versus open rotator cuff repair: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
authors: Zhang K, Wang Y, Chen L
abstract: PURPOSE: To compare clinical outcomes of arthroscopic versus open rotator cuff repair in adults. METHODS: We searched PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library for RCTs comparing arthroscopic and open rotator cuff repair in adults (>18 years). Primary outcomes were re-tear rate and functional scores. RESULTS: Twelve RCTs (n=1,245) were included. No significant difference in re-tear rates (RR 0.92, 95% CI 0.71-1.19). Arthroscopic repair showed superior Constant scores at 24 months (MD 3.2, p=0.03). CONCLUSIONS: Both techniques yield comparable outcomes in adults with full-thickness rotator cuff tears.

title: Platelet-rich plasma augmentation of rotator cuff repair: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
authors: Chen R, Li S, Wang H, Liu J
abstract: BACKGROUND: PRP augmentation during rotator cuff repair may improve healing. PURPOSE: To assess the effect of PRP on re-tear rates and functional outcomes after rotator cuff repair in adults. METHODS: Systematic review of RCTs and prospective cohort studies (n>20) comparing rotator cuff repair with vs without PRP augmentation. RESULTS: Twenty-two studies (n=1,876) were included. PRP reduced re-tear rates (OR 0.64, 95% CI 0.47-0.88, p=0.006) but did not significantly improve functional scores. CONCLUSION: PRP augmentation reduces re-tear rates in rotator cuff repair.

title: Biomechanical properties of suture anchors in rotator cuff repair: an in vitro cadaveric study.
authors: Park JH, Kim TK, Lee SH
journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
abstract: BACKGROUND: Suture anchor fixation strength is critical for rotator cuff repair durability. PURPOSE: To compare pullout strength of 3 suture anchor designs in cadaveric humeral heads. METHODS: Eighteen matched-pair cadaveric shoulders were used. Three anchor types were tested: all-suture, PEEK, and biocomposite. Maximum pullout force was measured. RESULTS: Biocomposite anchors showed highest mean pullout strength (412 ± 45 N), followed by PEEK (389 ± 52 N) and all-suture (298 ± 61 N). CONCLUSION: Biocomposite anchors provide superior fixation in a cadaveric model.

title: Rotator cuff tears in the pediatric population: a case series and review.
abstract: Rotator cuff tears in children and adolescents are rare. We present 8 cases of traumatic rotator cuff tears in patients aged 8-16 years treated at our institution over 10 years. All patients underwent surgical repair and achieved full recovery. We review the literature on pediatric rotator cuff pathology.

title: Diabetes mellitus and rotator cuff repair outcomes: a systematic review.
authors: Lee MJ, Park JS, Kim YH, Choi WR
journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
abstract: BACKGROUND: Diabetes mellitus is a common comorbidity in patients undergoing rotator cuff repair. PURPOSE: To evaluate the impact of diabetes on rotator cuff repair outcomes. METHODS: Systematic review of studies comparing clinical outcomes and re-tear rates in diabetic versus non-diabetic adults after rotator cuff repair. RESULTS: Fifteen studies (n=4,567) were included. Diabetic patients had higher re-tear rates (OR 1.89, 95% CI 1.42-2.51) and lower functional scores. CONCLUSION: Diabetes adversely affects rotator cuff repair outcomes in adults.

title: Canine rotator cuff model for tendon healing research: a translational approach.
authors: Wilson D, Thompson R, Baker S
abstract: The canine infraspinatus tendon model is widely used for rotator cuff healing research. We describe a refined surgical technique for creating controlled rotator cuff tears in 12 adult beagles. Healing was assessed histologically at 6 and 12 weeks. This model provides a reproducible platform for evaluating biological augmentation strategies.

title: Return to sport after rotator cuff repair in overhead athletes: a cohort study.
authors: Nakamura T, Sato K, Yamamoto A, Ishikawa H
abstract: BACKGROUND: Return to sport (RTS) after rotator cuff repair in overhead athletes is poorly understood. PURPOSE: To assess RTS rates and timing in adult overhead athletes after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair. METHODS: Prospective cohort of 89 overhead athletes (mean age 32, range 18-52) with full-thickness rotator cuff tears who underwent arthroscopic repair. Minimum 2-year follow-up. RESULTS: 78% returned to sport at a mean of 8.2 months. Younger age and smaller tear size predicted successful RTS. CONCLUSION: Most overhead athletes return to sport after rotator cuff repair.

title: Cost-effectiveness of surgical vs conservative treatment for rotator cuff tears: an editorial.
journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery

title: Shoulder disorders including rotator cuff disease: prevalence and comorbidities in a primary care population.
authors: Anderson J, Williams K, Taylor M, Brown P
abstract: BACKGROUND: Shoulder disorders are common in primary care. Comorbid conditions may influence treatment outcomes. METHODS: Cross-sectional study of 2,345 adults presenting with shoulder pain in 12 primary care clinics. We assessed the prevalence of rotator cuff disease and associated comorbidities. RESULTS: Rotator cuff disease was diagnosed in 42% (n=985). Common comorbidities included hypertension (38%), diabetes (22%), obesity (31%), and depression (18%). Patients with ≥2 comorbidities had worse functional scores. CONCLUSION: Comorbidities are highly prevalent in adults with rotator cuff disease and should be addressed in treatment planning.

title: Adhesive capsulitis of the shoulder: diagnosis and management.
abstract: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is a common cause of shoulder pain and stiffness. This review summarizes current evidence on diagnosis and management. We focus on the differentiation from rotator cuff pathology and discuss conservative and surgical treatment options. Adhesive capsulitis typically resolves within 1-3 years, though some patients develop persistent restriction.
